revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[bo:དཀྱུས་དམངས་]][[el:Κοινός άνθρωπος]][[en:Commoner]][[eo:Homamaso]][[es:Masas]][[fo:Almúgan]][[fr:Roturier]][[hi:जनसाधारण]][[id:Rakyat jelata]][[it:Roturier]][[ja:平民]][[ko:평민]][[ru:Массы]][[simple:Commoners]][[sv:Borgerlig släkt]][[uk:Маси]][[vi:Thường dân]][[zh:平民]]
'''Heimin''' (平民) refers to the ordinary (common) people or citizens who do not hold any official Rank (官位) or title of nobility (爵位). It is often contrasted with the aristocracy (貴族).
== Modern Japan == In Japan, the term "Heimin" was introduced as a legal term after the societal reorganization following the Meiji Restoration. In 1869 (Meiji 2), families of the imperial court and daimyō were designated as {{ill|Kazoku|en|Kazoku|qid=Q241734|lt=Kazoku|1=Kazoku}}, while samurai with official Ranks became {{ill|Shizoku|en|Shizoku|qid=Q3482406|lt=Shizoku|1=Shizoku}}, and lower-Ranking samurai, such as ashigaru, became {{ill|Sotsuzoku|ja|卒族|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sotsuzoku|1=sotsuzoku|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11407147}}. The general populace was designated as "Heimin."
In May 1872 (Meiji 5), the Dajōkan Proclamation No. 29 abolished the Sotsuzoku category, and by March 1875 (Meiji 8), Proclamation No. 44 stated that "citizens' signatures and titles must indicate whether they are Kazoku, Shizoku, or Heimin." Thus, Heimin became the legal term for those categorized alongside Kazoku and Shizoku.<ref>古川亮平「近代日本における旧身分意識と族称 -士族・平民の廃止について-」『駿台史學』174号、142-143頁.</ref> During the reorganization of Japan's social class system under the principles of {{ill|Four occupations|ja|四民平等|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Four occupations|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 士農工商|1=Four occupations|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q1328489}} and the {{ill|Chitsuroku-shobun|ja|秩禄処分|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chitsuroku-shobun|1=Abolition of Stipends|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11596176}}, Heimin comprised 93.41% of the total population, or 31,106,514 people.<ref>平野義太郎『日本資本主義社会の機構』</ref>
Individuals born into Kazoku or Shizoku families who established independent households without inheriting their family's status were generally reclassified as Heimin. A notable example is {{ill|Hara Takashi|en|Hara Takashi|qid=Q315553|lt=Hara Takashi|1=Hara Takashi}}, known as the "commoner prime minister," who was born the son of a retainer to the Morioka domain's chief retainer but became Heimin. Exceptions included cases where Kazoku families adopted individuals or granted special permission for family division within Kazoku.
The legal distinction between Shizoku and Heimin pertained solely to the designation of lineage and conferred no special privileges. Kazoku, however, were granted certain privileges under public law, such as the {{ill|Kazoku-rei|ja|華族令|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kazoku-rei|1=Peerage Law|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11620084}}, but were otherwise treated similarly to Shizoku and Heimin.
From March 1875, while Kazoku and Shizoku were explicitly indicated on family registers, Heimin was not. In 1938 (Shōwa 13), the term "Heimin" was removed from official records,<ref>『富山県民とともに 北日本新聞100th year 史』（1984th year 10月15日、北日本新聞社発行）224ページ.</ref> and in 1947 (Shōwa 22), the {{ill|Constitution of Japan|en|Constitution of Japan|qid=Q237082|lt=Constitution of Japan|1=Constitution of Japan}} Article 14 abolished the Kazoku, Shizoku, and Heimin distinctions under the principle of equality before the law.
== Western Context ==
=== France === Under the {{ill|Ancien régime|en|Ancien régime|qid=Q234081|lt=Ancien Régime|1=Ancien régime}}, commoners were referred to as the "Third Estate" (第三身分), distinct from the clergy (第一身分) and nobility (第二身分).
=== United Kingdom === In the United Kingdom, "commoners" are defined as those who are not the monarch or peers (nobles with titles such as Duke, Earl, etc.). Notably: * '''Courtesy titles''' do not confer noble status. For example, family members of a titleholder are commoners unless they hold titles themselves. * '''Baronets and Knights''', while titled, are legally commoners as they are not considered peers. - Royal family members without a peerage title, such as Prince William before being granted the title of Duke of Cambridge, are technically commoners.<ref>{{cite news|url=https://www.afpbb.com/articles/-/2797682?pid=7147728|title=ウィリアム王子はケンブリッジ公爵、ケイトさんはケンブリッジ公爵夫人に|date=2011-04-29|publisher=AFPBB NEWS|accessdate=2012-05-14}}</ref> However, members of the royal family often enjoy privileges like {{ill|Diplomatic immunity|en|Diplomatic immunity|qid=Q753022|lt=diplomatic immunity|1=Diplomatic immunity}}, distinguishing them from ordinary commoners.
=== Ancient Rome === {{main|Plebeians}} In the {{ill|Roman Republic|en|Roman Republic|qid=Q17167|lt=Roman Republic|1=Roman Republic}}, citizens who were not part of the patrician (貴族) class were known as plebeians (プレブス). '''Slaves were not included among plebeians'''.
== See Also == * {{ill|Four occupations|en|Four occupations|qid=Q1328489|lt=Four occupations|1=Four occupations}} * {{ill|Jinshin Koseki|ja|壬申戸籍|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jinshin Koseki|1=jinshin koseki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11429522}} * {{ill|Kazoku|en|Kazoku|qid=Q241734|lt=Kazoku|1=Kazoku}} * {{ill|Shizoku|en|Shizoku|qid=Q3482406|lt=Shizoku|1=Shizoku}} * {{ill|Sotsuzoku|ja|卒族|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sotsuzoku|1=sotsuzoku|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11407147}} * {{ill|New Commoners|ja|新平民|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/New Commoners|1=shin-heimin|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11502136}} * {{ill|Proletariat|en|Proletariat|qid=Q132603|lt=Proletariat|1=Proletariat}}
{{history-stub}}
